docs.title: Chrome with SOCKS v5 proxy
docs.text: These are the settings I use when I want Chrome to go through a SOCKS v5 proxy.

docs.title: Why I use this
docs.text: • I work a lot with SSH tunnels and needed a way to access different GUIs in the browser through them.

docs.title: Launch Chrome with a SOCKS proxy
docs.text:
To send Chrome traffic through a SOCKS v5 proxy on myproxy:8080, start Chrome with:

--proxy-server="socks5://myproxy:8080"
--host-resolver-rules="MAP * 0.0.0.0 , EXCLUDE myproxy"

docs.title: What to check when debugging
docs.text:
The first thing I usually check is the Proxy tab in Chrome's net internals page to see what Chrome is actually using:

  • chrome://net-internals/#proxy

Then I check the DNS tab to make sure Chrome is not doing local DNS lookups:

  • chrome://net-internals/#dns

If I want to trace how Chrome handled a specific request, I use the Events tab:

  • chrome://net-internals/#events

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Create a local SOCKS proxy with SSH:

ssh -C2qTnN -D 8080 user@domain.com

This command opens an SSH connection and creates a local SOCKS proxy on 127.0.0.1:8080.

The flags mean:

  • -C enables compression
  • -2 forces SSH protocol version 2
  • -q keeps the output quiet
  • -T disables pseudo-terminal allocation
  • -n redirects stdin from /dev/null
  • -N does not run a remote command
  • -D 8080 creates the local SOCKS proxy on port 8080

Then launch Chrome with:

google-chrome --proxy-server="socks5://127.0.0.1:8080" --host-resolver-rules="MAP * ~NOTFOUND , EXCLUDE 127.0.0.1"

docs.title: My setup
docs.text:
On macOS I use:

open -a Google\ Chrome --args --proxy-server="socks5://127.0.0.1:8080" --host-resolver-rules="MAP * ~NOTFOUND , EXCLUDE 127.0.0.1"

docs.title: NET::ERR_CERT_INVALID
docs.text:
If Chrome shows NET::ERR_CERT_INVALID, there is a hidden bypass on the error page. Click anywhere on the page and type:

thisisunsafe

I would only use that for testing in an environment I trust.

docs.title: OpenShift and Kubernetes
docs.text: Posts and notes related to OpenShift and Kubernetes.

docs.title: Posts
docs.text:
• Manage users with htpasswd
  • Running Squid in a container

docs.title: Manage users with htpasswd in OpenShift
docs.text: These are the commands I use when I need to manage local users with htpasswd in OpenShift.

docs.title: Create a new htpasswd file
docs.text:
This creates a new file and adds a user called new_admin:

htpasswd -c -B -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd new_admin redhat

The important part here is -c, since that creates a new file. If the file already exists, it will be overwritten.

docs.title: Create the secret used by OAuth
docs.text: oc create secret generic localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

docs.title: Restart OAuth
docs.text: oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication

docs.title: Give the user cluster-admin
docs.text: oc adm policy add-cluster-role-to-user cluster-admin new_admin

docs.title: Add a user to an existing htpasswd setup
docs.text:
If the secret already exists, extract the current file first:

oc extract secret/localusers -n openshift-config --to ~/auth-providers/ --confirm

Then add another user:

htpasswd -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd manager redhat

Update the secret:

oc set data secret/localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

And restart OAuth again:

oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication

docs.title: Running Squid in a container
docs.text:
I had been running Squid on a physical machine before, but I wanted to move it into a container to make it easier to manage and deploy.

I used to run Squid on some old leftover hardware, then I started thinking: why not just run it as a container or pod instead? In my case, I have an OpenShift cluster but you can run it on vanilla Kubernetes or directly in Docker or Podman if you wanted to.

These notes are based on the files in my squid-proxy folder. That is enough to understand how the proxy is configured and deployed, even though the actual Dockerfile and entrypoint.sh are not included.

docs.title: What is in the project
docs.text:
The project is split into a few parts:

  • squid.conf for the main Squid configuration
  • mime.conf for MIME type mappings
  • configmap.yaml to mount the config into the container
  • deploy.yaml for the Squid deployment
  • svc.yaml to expose the proxy on port 3128
  • ingress.yaml for HTTP ingress
  • squid-scc.yaml for OpenShift security context constraints
  • README with basic Docker build and run commands

docs.title: Building the container
docs.text:
The project README shows a simple Docker build and push flow:

docker build -t squid-proxy:v1.0 --omit-history .
docker push squid-proxy:v1.0

The README also shows how the image was tested interactively:

docker run -it localhost/squid-proxy:v1.0 /bin/bash

The actual Dockerfile or Containerfile is not in this folder, so this page is mostly about how the proxy is configured and deployed rather than how the image is built internally.

docs.title: Squid config
docs.text:
There are two places where the Squid config shows up in this project:

  • a standalone squid.conf
  • an embedded version inside configmap.yaml

The general pattern is the same in both places: Squid listens on 3128, defines ACLs, and uses local disk for cache and runtime data.

For example:

http_port 3128
cache_dir ufs /var/spool/squid 100 16 256

A few things that stand out in the config:

  • local source networks are defined with acl localnet
  • safe ports and CONNECT ACLs are defined
  • local domains and specific destination networks are allowed directly
  • all other traffic can be forwarded through an upstream proxy with cache_peer

The config also references writable paths like:

  • /var/spool/squid
  • /var/log/squid
  • /var/run/squid

From the manifests, I can clearly see storage mounted for /var/spool/squid. The log and runtime paths are in the config too, but how those are prepared inside the container depends on the image and entrypoint, and those files are not part of this folder.

docs.title: How the config gets into the container
docs.text:
The Kubernetes setup uses a ConfigMap called squid-config and mounts squid.conf into the container:

volumes:
  - name: squid-config
    configMap:
      name: squid-config

and:

volumeMounts:
  - name: squid-config
    mountPath: /etc/squid/squid.conf
    subPath: squid.conf

There is also a second ConfigMap for mime.conf.

That makes it easier to update the proxy config without rebuilding the image.

docs.title: Running the container
docs.text:
The README shows the image being tested locally with:

docker run -it localhost/squid-proxy:v1.0 /bin/bash

That part is the same no matter where you run it. If the image works, it can run with Docker, Podman, Kubernetes, OpenShift, or any other platform that supports containers.

docs.title: Deploying it on Kubernetes or OpenShift
docs.text:
The deployment runs a single Squid replica and starts it through an entrypoint script:

containers:
  - name: squid
    image: squid-proxy:v1.0
    command: ["/bin/bash", "-c", "/usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh"]

The container exposes port 3128:

ports:
  - containerPort: 3128
    protocol: TCP

The deployment also mounts an emptyDir volume to /var/spool/squid, which is where Squid keeps cache data in this setup.

docs.title: Service exposure
docs.text:
The Service exposes Squid as a LoadBalancer on port 3128:

spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
    - port: 3128
      targetPort: 3128

There is also an ingress manifest in the project, but for a forward proxy the LoadBalancer Service is the part that matters most.

docs.title: OpenShift-specific setup
docs.text:
There is an SCC manifest called squid-scc.yaml for OpenShift. It allows the container to run with the security settings this image expects.

From the deployment:

  • a dedicated service account is used
  • fsGroup: 999 is set
  • allowPrivilegeEscalation: true is enabled in the container security context

I do not see the ServiceAccount manifest itself in this folder, but the deployment clearly expects one named squid-proxy.

From that, it looks like the deployment was adjusted so Squid would run properly inside OpenShift with writable mounted paths and a custom runtime user setup.

docs.title: PVCs vs current deployment
docs.text:
The project also includes pvc.yaml for cache and logs, but the current deployment manifest uses emptyDir instead of the PVCs.

So at the moment:

  • cache is ephemeral
  • the pod can be recreated without keeping cached data

If persistent cache or logs are important, the deployment would need to mount those PVCs instead of using emptyDir.

docs.title: What this setup looks like in practice
docs.text:
In practice, the setup is basically this:

  1. Build and push a Squid container image
  2. Put squid.conf and mime.conf in ConfigMaps
  3. Deploy the container with writable mounted storage
  4. Expose it with a Service on port 3128
  5. Add the OpenShift-specific security settings if running on OpenShift

The part I like most with this setup is that the proxy config is kept outside the container image. That makes it easier to adjust ACLs, upstream proxy settings, and local domain rules without rebuilding the image each time.

docs.title: Single node OpenShift running on OpenShift Virtualization
docs.text: A quite large project to get Single node OpenShift to run inside a VM on OpenShift Virtualization. The intention is to more effectivly using the hardware but also enabling an aaS concept with Single node OpenShift

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before proceeding, its recommened to use the following: - ## Hardware: Ensure that your system is running one of the following: - Any server with atleast 192GB of memory. - Intel Xenon 6132 CPU - Intel xxv710 or Intel E810 NIC

  • Admin rights to install required software

  • Access to the project repository

  • Stable internet connection

  • Access to required internal/external resources

  • Docker (if containerization is used)
  • Virtual environment (recommended for Python projects)
  htpasswd -c -B -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd new_admin redhat
⚠ Caution: Running this command with the `-c` option will overwrite the existing htpasswd file. Make sure you do not lose existing user data.

docs.title: Operating System:
docs.text:
• Red Hat OpenShift 4.15 or later.
  • OpenShift Virtualization x.zz
  • List of operators TBA

docs.text: oc create secret generic localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

docs.title: Rollout deployment to apply configuration
docs.text: oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication

docs.title: Add cluster-admin to user
docs.text: oc adm policy add-cluster-role-to-user cluster-admin new_admin

docs.title: Add a user to existing OAuth config
Extract the file data from secret
docs.text: oc extract secret/localusers -n openshift-config --to ~/auth-providers/ --confirm

docs.title: Add new user to htpasswd called manager with password redhat
docs.text: htpasswd -b ~/auth-providers/htpasswd manager redhat

docs.title: Update secret used by OAuth
docs.text: oc set data secret/localusers --from-file htpasswd=~/auth-providers/htpasswd -n openshift-config

docs.title: Rollout deployment to apply configuration
docs.text: oc rollout restart deployment/oauth-openshift -n openshift-authentication
